source.zh: George Grey《Polynesian Mythology》（1855；毛利语本 1854 年以《Ko nga Mahinga a nga Tupuna Maori》刊行，底本主要是 Te Arawa 的 Wiremu Te Rangikāheke 手稿）、John White《The Ancient History of the Maori》六卷（1887–1890）、Elsdon Best《Maori Religion and Mythology》（Dominion Museum Bulletin, 1924）、Percy Smith 编《The Lore of the Whare-wānanga》（Memoirs of the Polynesian Society, 1913–1915）、Teuira Henry《Ancient Tahiti》（Bishop Museum Bulletin 48, 1928，据 J. M. Orsmond 手稿）、Martha Beckwith《Hawaiian Mythology》（1940）与《The Kumulipo: A Hawaiian Creation Chant》（1951）、Davida Malo《Hawaiian Antiquities》（英译本 1903）、Kumulipo 夏威夷语本（Kalākaua 刊印，1889）与 Liliʻuokalani 英译本（1897）
source.en: George Grey, Polynesian Mythology (1855; the Māori text published in 1854 as Ko nga Mahinga a nga Tupuna Maori, drawn largely from the manuscripts of Wiremu Te Rangikāheke of Te Arawa); John White, The Ancient History of the Maori, 6 vols (1887–1890); Elsdon Best, Maori Religion and Mythology (Dominion Museum Bulletin, 1924); S. Percy Smith, ed., The Lore of the Whare-wānanga (Memoirs of the Polynesian Society, 1913–1915); Teuira Henry, Ancient Tahiti (Bishop Museum Bulletin 48, 1928, from the manuscripts of J. M. Orsmond); Martha Beckwith, Hawaiian Mythology (1940) and The Kumulipo: A Hawaiian Creation Chant (1951); Davida Malo, Hawaiian Antiquities (English translation, 1903); the Kumulipo in Hawaiian, printed by Kalākaua (1889), and Liliʻuokalani's English translation (1897)

note.zh:
「波利尼西亚神话」是个伞形称呼。从夏威夷到新西兰再到复活节岛，横跨半个太平洋，语言同源、神名可以逐字对上（Tāne=Kāne，Tangaroa=Taʻaroa=Kanaloa=Tagaloa，Tū=Kū，Rongo=Lono），故事却各走各的路，没有共同的定本。这条时间线走毛利（Aotearoa）一路，因为毛利的这一套在十九世纪被记得最全；记录得全，跟哪一套更「正宗」是两回事。

即便在毛利内部也没有定本。今天全世界转述的「朗吉与帕帕」版本，主要来自 Te Arawa 的 Wiremu Te Rangikāheke 交给殖民地总督 George Grey 的手稿；Grey 作了编辑与重排，1854 年出毛利语本、1855 年出英译。换句话说，被当成「毛利创世神话」的东西，源头是一个部族、一个人的讲法，经一个外人之手整理成书。John White 的六卷本里收着大量互相打架的部族异传：撑开天地的未必是塔内，朗吉与帕帕的子女数目各说不一，第一个人是塔内在 Kurawaka 用红土造的 Hine-ahu-one，还是 Tiki 本人就是第一个人（或造人者），各部族的答案不同。至上神 Io 的材料主要出自 Te Matorohanga 一系、经 Percy Smith 于 1913–1915 年刊布，它究竟是接触前的古老秘传还是传教后的晚近整合，学界争论已久，此处不把 Io 写进主线。

坦加罗阿的地位在各岛之间整个翻转。在毛利这里，他是被塔怀里马特阿的风暴赶下海底的失败者；在塔希提（Teuira Henry 据 Orsmond 手稿），Taʻaroa 独自坐在名叫 Rumia 的贝壳里，在虚空中旋转了不知多久，破壳而出：他把自己待过的那只壳 Rumia 翻上去盖成天穹，另一只壳摊开作大地的岩层与土壤，再拿自己的身体做世界——羽毛化为草木藤蔓，脊骨化为山脉，脏腑化为流云，肠子化为龙虾、虾与鳗；在萨摩亚和汤加，Tagaloa 是天上的造物主。同一个名字，一处是造世者，一处是败军之将。

夏威夷的 Kumulipo 是另一条完全不同的路子：两千余行的宇宙谱系诵歌，分十六个 wā，前七个还在 pō（黑暗）里，后九个进入 ao（光）。它不讲哪位神推开天地，而是从最小的东西数起——最先生出来的是珊瑚虫——此后海里生一物、陆上就对生一物，名字成对（如海里的 ʻekaha 配岸上的 ʻekahakaha），这样一代代数到人。神与人在第八 wā 的破晓处一同出现：Laʻilaʻi 是女人，Kiʻi 是男人，Kāne 是神，Kanaloa 是章鱼。此本 1889 年由 Kalākaua 王刊印夏威夷语本，Liliʻuokalani 女王的英译于 1897 年出版。夏威夷另有 Wākea（天）与 Papahānaumoku（生出诸岛的地母）一系：两人生下女儿 Hoʻohōkūkalani；Wākea 与这个女儿所生的头一个孩子不足月，生下来没有活，埋在屋边，土里长出第一株芋头，名叫 Hāloanaka；第二个孩子取名 Hāloa，是夏威夷人的祖先（见 Davida Malo）——照这个讲法，芋头是人的兄长。这些是仍在延续的传统，此处按其自身的叙述陈述。

最后一点：十九世纪的记录本（毛利与夏威夷都有）有多少受了传教士接触的塑形，Beckwith 等人在讨论造人叙事时已经提出疑问，至今没有定论。
note.en:
Polynesian mythology is an umbrella term covering the traditions of a great many islands. Across half the Pacific, from Hawaiʻi to Aotearoa to Rapa Nui, the languages are cognate and the god-names line up sound for sound (Tāne = Kāne, Tangaroa = Taʻaroa = Kanaloa = Tagaloa, Tū = Kū, Rongo = Lono), but the stories diverge and there is no shared canon. This timeline follows the Māori line because Māori accounts were written down at the greatest narrative length in the nineteenth century. Fullness of record is a fact about the records; it settles nothing about which tradition is older or truer.

Even within Māori tradition there is no fixed text. The version of Rangi and Papa retold worldwide comes chiefly from manuscripts written for the colonial governor George Grey by Wiremu Te Rangikāheke of Te Arawa; Grey edited and rearranged them, publishing the Māori text in 1854 and an English rendering in 1855. What circulates as 'the Māori creation myth' is therefore one man's account, from one iwi, shaped by an outsider's editing. John White's six volumes preserve tribal versions that contradict it and each other: the separator of sky and earth is not always Tāne, the number of the children varies, and accounts differ over whether the first human was Hine-ahu-one, formed by Tāne from red earth at Kurawaka, or Tiki, who is himself the first man in some traditions and the maker of the first man in others. The supreme being Io comes mainly from the teachings of Te Matorohanga published by Percy Smith in 1913–1915, and whether Io is pre-contact esoteric lore or a later synthesis shaped by missionary contact has been argued for a century. Io is not built into the main sequence here.

Tangaroa's rank flips from island to island. In Aotearoa he is the brother driven down into the sea by Tāwhirimātea's storms. In Tahiti, in the material Teuira Henry took from Orsmond's manuscripts, Taʻaroa sits alone inside a shell called Rumia, turning in the void. He cracks it open, lifts the shell he had been living in to be the dome of the sky, spreads the second shell out for the rock and soil of the earth, and makes the rest of the world from his own body: his feathers become the trees and creepers, his backbone a mountain range, his vitals the drifting clouds, his intestines the lobsters and shrimps and eels. In Samoa and Tonga, Tagaloa is the sky-dwelling creator. One name, creator in one archipelago and loser in another.

The Hawaiian Kumulipo takes a different road entirely. It is a genealogical chant of more than two thousand lines in sixteen wā, the first seven still in pō, the darkness, the last nine in ao, the light. No god pushes sky and earth apart in it. It counts upward from the smallest thing: the coral polyp is born first, and thereafter each sea creature is born with a land plant paired to it, the names matching as partners (the ʻekaha in the sea, the ʻekahakaha on shore), generation after generation until human beings arrive. Gods and people appear together at the dawn of the eighth wā, where Laʻilaʻi is born a woman, Kiʻi a man, Kāne a god, and Kanaloa the octopus. Kalākaua printed the Hawaiian text in 1889 and Liliʻuokalani's English translation appeared in 1897. Hawaiʻi also holds the line of Wākea, the sky, and Papahānaumoku, the earth mother who bore the islands. Their daughter is Hoʻohōkūkalani, and the first child Wākea has by that daughter is born untimely and does not live; it is buried beside the house, and from the grave grows the first taro plant, Hāloanaka. The second child is named Hāloa and is the ancestor of the Hawaiian people, which makes taro the elder sibling of humankind (Davida Malo). These are living traditions and are reported here as they are told.

One caution: nineteenth-century records from both Aotearoa and Hawaiʻi may carry the imprint of missionary contact, a question Beckwith herself raised about accounts of the making of the first humans, and one that is still open.

title.zh: 先是虚无，然后是数不清的夜
title.en: The nothing, then the long tally of nights

text.zh: 开头这一段的形式是谱系。诵者从 Te Kore 数起——「无」，什么都没有，连可以生出东西的空处都还没有——再一段段数下去：Te Kore 之后是一串各有名字的 Te Pō，长夜、大夜、深夜、漆黑之夜、可摸得着的夜，一夜生一夜，像人报祖先的名字那样一路报下来，末了才到 Te Ao Mārama，光明的世界。这套数法在各部族的 whakapapa 里长短不一，名目也对不齐，但走向一样：从无到暗，从暗到光。
text.en: The opening comes in the form of a genealogy. The reciter begins at Te Kore, the nothing, where not even the space in which something might happen exists yet, and works forward through a chain of named nights, Te Pō in its many forms, the long night, the great night, the dark night, the night that can be felt, each night begetting the next in the way a man recites his ancestors, until the count arrives at Te Ao Mārama, the world of light. Different iwi recite the chain at different lengths and the names do not match up between them, but the direction is the same: nothing, then darkness, then light.

title.zh: 父母抱得太紧，孩子在中间摸黑长大
title.en: The parents cling, and the children grow up in the dark between them

text.zh: 长夜数到尽头，出现的是朗吉努伊（Ranginui，天）与帕帕图阿努库（Papatūānuku，地）——光明的世界要等到他们被分开才有。两人紧紧相拥，中间不留缝隙，生下的孩子只能在父母的身体之间挤着长大，从没见过光。Grey 记下的六个儿子是：塔内马胡塔（森林与鸟）、坦加罗阿（海与鱼）、图马塔乌恩加（人与战争）、朗戈马塔内（人工栽培的食物，甘薯）、豪米亚提凯提凯（野生的食物，蕨根）、塔怀里马特阿（风与暴雨）。别的部族记的子女数目远不止这几个。他们在黑暗里摸索着彼此，商量：难道要一辈子这样过下去？
text.en: At the end of the nights come Ranginui the sky and Papatūānuku the earth, locked in an embrace so close that no gap is left between them; the world of light has to wait until they are parted. Their children are born into that gap and grow up crushed inside it, groping in the dark, having never seen light. The six sons named in Grey's text are Tāne-mahuta, of the forests and the birds; Tangaroa, of the sea and the fish; Tū-matauenga, of war and of human beings; Rongo-mā-tāne, of cultivated food, the kūmara; Haumia-tiketike, of wild food, the fernroot; and Tāwhirimātea, of wind and storm. Other iwi count far more children than six. In the dark the brothers feel for one another and begin to talk: are they to live like this forever?

title.zh: 塔内以头抵地、以脚蹬天
title.en: Tāne sets his head on his mother and his feet against his father

text.zh: 诸子开会。图马塔乌恩加主张干脆把父母杀掉。塔内马胡塔说不必：把他们分开就行——让天离得远远的作外人，让地留在下面作母亲。除塔怀里马特阿外，所有人都同意。动手的顺序是：朗戈先来，撑不动；坦加罗阿来，撑不动；豪米亚来，撑不动；图马塔乌恩加使尽力气，还是撑不动。最后是塔内。他先用手臂去掰，掰不开；于是停下来，把头和肩顶在母亲身上，两脚抵住父亲，慢慢把腰背和腿一寸寸伸直。天与地被撕开了，两人齐声哀号。光第一次照进这个空处。塔内立起支柱把天撑住，因此他有个名号叫 Tāne-te-toko-o-te-rangi——撑天的塔内。
text.en: The brothers hold a council. Tū-matauenga proposes killing the parents outright. Tāne-mahuta says there is no need: part them, and let the sky stand far off as a stranger while the earth stays below as a mother. Every brother agrees except Tāwhirimātea. They try in order. Rongo-mā-tāne heaves and fails. Tangaroa heaves and fails. Haumia-tiketike fails. Tū-matauenga strains with all his strength and fails. Then Tāne. He strains at them with his hands and arms first and cannot move them, so he stops, plants his head and shoulders against his mother, braces his feet on his father, and straightens his back and legs slowly, inch by inch, until sky and earth tear apart and both cry out. Light enters the gap for the first time. Tāne sets props under the sky to hold it up, and from this he takes the name Tāne-te-toko-o-te-rangi, Tāne who props the heavens.

title.zh: 塔内把日月和星子钉在父亲胸口
title.en: Tāne fastens the sun, the moon and the stars on his father

text.zh: 天空撑起来了，还是空的。塔内去取来日和月，把它们安在父亲身上，又把星子一颗颗缀上去；地上则由他铺开森林，从此那片林子叫 te wao nui a Tāne——塔内的大森林。分开之后两人并没有停止思念：帕帕图阿努库的叹息作为雾气从地面往上升，朗吉努伊的眼泪落下来就是露水，天冷时下得更多。有的部族说，诸子后来把母亲翻过身去、面朝下，免得两人日夜对望着哭。
text.en: The sky is propped, but it is bare. Tāne goes to fetch the sun and the moon and sets them on his father, and hangs the stars on him one by one. Below, he clothes his mother with forest, and to this day the great bush is called te wao nui a Tāne, the great forest of Tāne. Separation does not end the longing. The mist that rises off the ground is Papatūānuku sighing upward, and the dew that settles is Ranginui weeping, heaviest in the cold. Some iwi add that the sons afterwards turned their mother face downward, so that the two would not lie staring at each other and grieving.

title.zh: 风神打服四个兄弟，图吃掉他们
title.en: The storm beats four brothers, and Tū eats them

text.zh: 塔怀里马特阿从头到尾反对分开父母。他跟着父亲上了天，在那里养大自己的儿女——各路狂风、飓风、暴雨、密云，然后放它们下来。先打塔内的森林：大树被拦腰折断，倒在地上等虫蛀烂。再打坦加罗阿的海：浪掀起来，坦加罗阿逃进深处。坦加罗阿的孙辈就在这时分了家——伊卡特雷（Ikatere）带鱼群逃进海里，图特威威（Tū-te-wehiwehi）带爬虫逃进森林投奔塔内；坦加罗阿从此恨塔内收留他的逃兵，所以到今天还翻船，而塔内则把木头给了人做独木舟、鱼钩和渔网，让人去海上跟他算账。朗戈与豪米亚被母亲藏进身体里，风找不着他们。只有图马塔乌恩加站着没动。风停之后，图找兄弟们算账：他们没一个帮他。他编套子捉塔内的鸟，用亚麻结网捞坦加罗阿的鱼，用尖头掘棒（ko）把朗戈和豪米亚从土里挖出来，晾干、吃掉，并念咒把他们变成寻常食物。人吃鸟、吃鱼、吃甘薯、吃蕨根的权利，是这样来的。
text.en: Tāwhirimātea never agreed to the parting. He follows his father upward and there raises his own offspring, the winds and squalls and hurricanes and the thick clouds, and then looses them. First he strikes the forests of Tāne: the great trees snap and lie where they fall for the insects to eat. Then he strikes the sea of Tangaroa, whipping it into waves until Tangaroa flees into the depths. It is now that Tangaroa's grandchildren split: Ikatere leads the fish into the sea, and Tū-te-wehiwehi leads the reptiles inland into the forest, where Tāne takes them in. Tangaroa has hated Tāne for sheltering his deserters ever since, which is why he still swamps canoes, and why Tāne gives the descendants of Tū wood for hulls and hooks and fibre for nets, so that they can go out and settle the score. Rongo and Haumia are hidden inside their mother's body and the wind cannot find them. Only Tū-matauenga stands his ground. When the storm dies, Tū turns on his brothers, none of whom stood with him. He snares the birds of Tāne, nets the fish of Tangaroa, digs Rongo and Haumia out of the ground with a wooden ko, dries them, eats them all, and by his chants renders them ordinary food. That is how people came by the right to eat birds, fish, kūmara and fernroot.

title.zh: 塔内用红土捏出第一个女人，对着鼻子吹气
title.en: Tāne shapes a woman from red earth and breathes into her nose

text.zh: 塔内想要后代，找遍了各样存在与之结合，生出来的却是溪流、石头、爬虫、草木，没有一个是人。母亲告诉他到 Kurawaka 去，那是她身上的一处，土是红的。他在那里把红土一捧捧堆起来，捏出一个女人的形体，俯身把鼻子贴上她的鼻子，把气送进去。她打了个喷嚏，活了过来。她叫 Hine-ahu-one，「用土堆成的女子」。塔内与她生下 Hine-tītama，破晓的女儿；人类从这里开始。别的部族不这么讲：有的说第一个人是 Tiki，有的说是 Tiki 捏的人，波利尼西亚各岛也大多把 Tiki 当作第一个人。
text.en: Tāne wants descendants. He mates with one being after another and gets streams, stones, reptiles and plants out of it, but no people. His mother tells him to go to Kurawaka, a place on her body where the earth is red. There he heaps the red soil up handful by handful and works it into the form of a woman, bends down, presses his nose to hers and breathes into her. She sneezes and comes alive. Her name is Hine-ahu-one, the woman heaped up from earth. Their daughter is Hine-tītama, the dawn maiden, and human beings descend from her. Other iwi tell it otherwise: some say the first man was Tiki himself, others that Tiki made the first human, and across most of Polynesia it is Tiki who is remembered as the first man.

title.zh: 祖母的下颌骨做钩，钓上来一条岛那么大的鱼
title.en: A grandmother's jawbone for a hook, and a fish the size of an island

text.zh: 毛伊拿祖母 Murirangawhenua 的下颌骨做了鱼钩。兄长们嫌他碍事，不肯带他出海，他就藏在独木舟的舱板底下，等船离岸太远、掉不了头了才钻出来。兄长们不给他鱼饵，他一拳打自己的鼻子，把血抹在钩上，抛下海去。钓上来的是一条极大的鱼——就是今天的北岛，Te Ika-a-Māui，「毛伊之鱼」；他的独木舟是南岛，Te Waka-a-Māui。他叫兄长们别动手，等他去请祭司来念完该念的经再分。他一走，兄长们就动刀去割那条鱼，鱼在痛里挣扎扭曲，砍出来的坑成了山谷，割不平的地方成了山脉与断崖——地面之所以不平整，就是因为他们没有等。夏威夷也有一条同样的钩，叫 Manaiakalani，毛伊本想把散开的岛拉到一处，兄弟回头看了一眼，钓线就断了。
text.en: Māui makes a hook from the jawbone of his grandmother Murirangawhenua. His brothers will not take him fishing, so he hides under the floorboards of the canoe and comes out only when they are too far from shore to turn back. They refuse him bait; he punches his own nose, smears the hook with his blood, and drops it. What he brings up is an enormous fish, and it is the North Island of New Zealand, Te Ika-a-Māui, the fish of Māui; his canoe is the South Island, Te Waka-a-Māui. He tells his brothers to leave the catch alone until he has fetched a priest to say the proper karakia over it. The moment he is gone they take their knives to it. The fish thrashes and writhes under the cutting, and the gouges they leave in it are the valleys, the ridges they hack are the mountains and the cliffs. The land is broken country because they would not wait. Hawaiʻi remembers the same hook under the name Manaiakalani, with which Māui tried to drag the scattered islands together until a brother looked back and the line parted.

title.zh: 扇尾鸟笑出了声，毛伊死在夜之女神体内
title.en: The fantail laughs, and Māui dies inside the goddess of night

text.zh: Hine-tītama长大后嫁给了塔内，后来问起父亲是谁，才知道丈夫就是父亲。她不再回到光里，走进下界，改名 Hine-nui-te-pō——大夜之女；她对塔内说，你在上面把孩子养大，我在下面把他们拉下来。人从此有死。毛伊想把这条规矩翻过来。他带着一群鸟去找她，她正睡着：眼睛是绿石，头发像海藻，牙齿是黑曜石。毛伊说，我从她两腿之间钻进去，从嘴里出来，她就死了，人就永生了；你们千万别笑，等我出来再笑。他钻进去一半，小小的扇尾鸟（tīwakawaka）实在忍不住，噗地笑出声。她醒了，一合，毛伊死在里面。人到今天还是会死。留下的世界就是 Te Ao Mārama，光明的世界；人在这里生老病死，死后灵魂回 Hawaiki——祖先的独木舟从那里划出来，人也从那里来，最后还回那里去。
text.en: Hine-tītama grows up and marries Tāne, and only later asks who her father is and learns that her husband is also her father. She does not come back into the light. She goes down below and takes a new name, Hine-nui-te-pō, great woman of the night, and tells Tāne to stay above and raise their children while she draws them down. From then on people die. Māui sets out to reverse it. He goes to her with a company of birds and finds her asleep: her eyes are greenstone, her hair is like kelp, her teeth are obsidian. He tells them his plan, to enter between her thighs and come out at her mouth, which will kill her and make people deathless, and warns them not to laugh until he is through. He is halfway in when the little fantail, the tīwakawaka, cannot hold it and bursts out laughing. She wakes, closes, and Māui dies inside her. People have died ever since. What is left is Te Ao Mārama, the world of light, where people are born and grow old and die, and where the spirits of the dead go back to Hawaiki, the homeland the ancestors' canoes came from.
